expectations.evidence: Puts Stripe in modules/billing/stripe.gateway.ts (inside the module), not src/adapters/. Wiring is vague: 'in your container/DI setup or a billing.plugin.ts.' The DevDigest rule (adapters live in src/adapters/, never in modules/) is missed.

expectations.text: Places proration as a pure domain function (no I/O)
expectations.evidence: Places proration in the service: 'Proration is a business rule, so it lives in the service, not the gateway and not the route.' Treated as application logic, never assigned to the domain layer.

expectations.text: Describes endpoints as thin handlers via getContext + one service call
expectations.evidence: Handlers are thin but workspaceId comes from client input (upgradeBody has workspaceId: z.string().uuid(); getSummary reads req.query.workspaceId). getContext is never used — and trusting a client-supplied workspaceId is itself a tenancy smell.

expectations.evidence: Puts domain types in modules/billing/billing.types.ts inside the module, not vendor/shared/contracts — which also blocks the client from importing them.

notes: Overall pass rate: with_skill 96% vs baseline 73% (+0.23). The gap is driven entirely by evals 0 and 2; evals 1 and 3 are ties.

Eval 0 (full-module review): with_skill 11/11 vs baseline 7/11. Baseline caught every GENERIC violation (direct DB, adapter-in-service, secret, cross-module repo, business-logic-in-route) but missed the four DevDigest-specific ones: $inferSelect/toDomain leak, getContext (demoted to a nit), missing workspace scope (a cross-tenant leak), and zod-in-domain (baseline even recommended KEEPING zod via z.infer).

Eval 2 (greenfield placement): with_skill 6/7 vs baseline 2/7 — the largest gap. Baseline put the Stripe adapter and domain types INSIDE the module (not src/adapters or vendor/shared), placed proration in the service instead of the domain, and sourced workspaceId from the client request body instead of getContext (a tenancy smell). The skill got each placement right.

Eval 1 (subtle single file) is NON-DISCRIMINATING: both configs scored 5/5. On a blatant, isolated violation the model's built-in architecture knowledge already catches everything; the skill adds precision/citations but no pass-rate delta.
Eval 3 (clean module) FALSE-POSITIVE CONTROL passed for both (3/3): neither config fabricated a violation on compliant code. The skill does not push the model into over-reporting.

The 'cross_module_via_service' assertion (eval 2, id 7) failed for BOTH configs because the single-new-module prompt never exercises cross-module access — it is a mis-targeted, non-discriminating assertion and should be dropped or moved to a multi-module eval.
Cost of the skill: +20.5s and +21k tokens on average per run (reading SKILL.md + rules files). Concentrated in eval 0 (184.8s) where the skill drove a far more thorough, rule-cited review.
